metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC1 to explicitly require separate test coverage for BOTH compact.py snapshot paths: `_compact_locked` AND `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log`, each asserting `author_email` in the resulting SNAPSHOT envelope.
findings.finding: AC1 requires testing 'a compact SNAPSHOT (compact.py)' but the plan identifies TWO distinct snapshot-composing code paths in compact.py (`_compact_locked` ~198 and `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` ~364). A single compact-path test could exercise only one of these paths and still satisfy the AC as written, leaving the other path unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 / What §2 compact.py bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC1's compact clause to: 'a compact SNAPSHOT via `_compact_locked` AND a SNAPSHOT rebuilt via `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log`, each asserting the resulting SNAPSHOT JSON contains `author_email`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the minimum N in AC5 (e.g., N ≥ 3) so the caching test is required to call `attribution_fields` multiple times and assert each counter equals exactly 1.
findings.finding: AC5 specifies 'across N sequential writes in one process for one repo_root' but leaves N undefined, making the cardinality assertion underspecified — an agent could write a test with N=1 that vacuously passes ('at most once' is trivially satisfied by a single call).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'N sequential writes' with 'at least 3 sequential calls to `attribution_fields` for the same repo_root' and assert each call-counter equals exactly 1 (not ≤ N).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC2) to verify that a post-change comment event reduces with `author_email` present in its per-entry dict, and similarly assert presence/absence for revert and signature records, mirroring the two-branch structure of AC2.
findings.finding: What §3 describes per-entry `author_email`/`author_id` surfacing for `process_comment`, `process_revert`, and `process_signature` processors, but no Acceptance Criterion verifies that these sub-record fields are correctly populated (or correctly absent on pre-change events). AC2 only checks top-level reduced state (process_create path); AC3 checks absence of new keys for pre-change events globally, but does not assert the per-entry fields appear when the keys ARE present in a comment/revert/signature event.
findings.location: What §3 (process_comment :248, process_revert :374, process_signature :494) vs Acceptance Criteria AC2 and AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2: 'With a current identity set: a COMMENT event's reduced per-entry record also surfaces `author_email`/`author_id`; a REVERT and a SIGNATURE record likewise. With no identity resolved, `author_id` is absent from all three sub-records.' This can be a single parametrized test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in §3 that the present-only read for comment/revert/signature sub-records uses `event.get('author_email')` / `event.get('author_id')` (envelope fields), NOT `data.get(...)` — and distinguish this explicitly from the `source_*` pattern which reads from `data`.
findings.finding: The plan's present-only surfacing rule for reducers (§3) says it 'mirrors `source_*`' but `source_*` fields are read from `data` (the event's data payload), while `author_email`/`author_id` are envelope fields stamped on the event itself (accessed via `event.get(...)`). The plan never explicitly states whether the reducer reads `event.get('author_email')` or `data.get('author_email')`. For the three sub-record processors (process_comment, process_revert, process_signature), the exact key-path protocol is unspecified — and the wrong choice silently produces an empty value on every event.
findings.location: §3 Replay back-compat — process_comment (:248), process_revert (:374), process_signature (:494)
findings.suggested_fix: In §3, replace 'mirrors source_*' with: 'reads from the event envelope (event.get("author_email"), event.get("author_id")), NOT from data — contrast with source_* which reads from data. Only set the key when the value is not None.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `attribution_fields` should omit `author_email` entirely when the value is empty (matching the `author_id` omit-when-None pattern), or explicitly document that `author_email: ''` is the canonical 'not configured' sentinel and update AC3 to cover new events written with no git email.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `attribution_fields()` always returns `{"author_email": author_email()}` — and `author_email()` returns `""` (never raises) when git config is unset. The guard `if attribution: event.update(attribution)` checks dict truthiness: `{"author_email": ""}` is a non-empty dict and is ALWAYS truthy, so new events will always get `author_email: ""` stamped even when git email is unset. This creates a silent forward-incompatibility: post-change events with no configured email carry the key with an empty value, while pre-change events have no key at all — a distinction AC3 doesn't test and downstream consumers (bde5, 3183) may not handle.
findings.location: Section 1 (`attribution_fields` spec), Section 2 (`if attribution: event.update(attribution)` guard), AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the author_id omit-when-None pattern: in `attribution_fields`, only include `author_email` when the value is non-empty (`if v: result['author_email'] = v`). This makes the guard `if attribution:` correctly skip the update when both email and author_id are absent, and keeps the envelope byte-shape consistent with pre-change events for the no-git-config case.

findings.finding: The plan says to call `attribution_fields(repo_root)` in `_compact_locked` and `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log`, but neither function receives a `repo_root` parameter — only `tracker` (the tracker directory path). The plan does not specify the derivation needed to obtain `repo_root` from `tracker`. The cache key is `os.path.realpath(str(repo_root or config.repo_root()))` — for the cache to coalesce correctly with `append_event`'s default-None path, the compact call must pass `os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(tracker))` (or equivalent). An implementer who passes `tracker` directly as `repo_root` would get a different cache key and break the 'at most once' guarantee.
findings.location: Section 2 — compact.py sites; also Section 1 — CACHE description
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan's compact site description: 'Derive `repo_root = os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(tracker))` before calling `attribution_fields(repo_root)`, matching the canonical derivation used in transition_close.py.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `repo_root` is obtained inside `_compact_locked` and `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` (e.g. derive via `os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(tracker))`, or add a `repo_root` param and update callers), and update the plan to make this explicit.
findings.finding: The plan instructs computing `attribution_fields(repo_root)` 'alongside' `_git_author()` in `_compact_locked` and `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log`, but neither function receives a `repo_root` parameter — `_compact_locked(tracker, ticket_id, ticket_dir, threshold, no_commit, horizon=0, emit_legacy_mirror=False)` and `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log(tracker, ticket_id, ticket_dir, *, no_commit=False, emit_legacy_mirror=None)` take `tracker` (a path string) but not `repo_root`. The plan does not specify how `repo_root` is obtained inside these functions or whether the signature should be extended.
findings.location: Section 2 (Stamp at ALL FOUR envelope-composing sites) — `compact.py` bullet; `file_impact` entry for `compact.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Derive repo_root from tracker inside both functions: `repo_root = os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(tracker))`, then call `attribution_fields(repo_root)`. This avoids a signature change and is consistent with how `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` already derives `_root = os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(tracker))` for config loading (line 331).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `_compact_locked` and `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` derive `repo_root` as `os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(tracker))` and pass that to `attribution_fields(repo_root)`, keeping the cache key consistent with `append_event`'s path.
findings.finding: The plan specifies calling `attribution_fields(repo_root)` inside `_compact_locked` and `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log`, but neither function has a `repo_root` parameter — only a `tracker` string. The plan does not specify that `repo_root` must be derived as `os.path.dirname(tracker)` (or equivalent) at these call sites, leaving the implementer to guess the derivation and risking an incorrect `config.repo_root()` fallback when the process CWD differs from the physical repo root.
findings.location: Section 2 — `_commands/compact.py` stamping paragraph; `_compact_locked` (~line 191) and `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` (~line 357)
findings.suggested_fix: Add one line before the `attribution_fields(...)` call in each compact function: `repo_root = os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(tracker))`, then pass it as `attribution_fields(repo_root)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit exception-handling rule to the `attribution_fields()` spec: wrap the `resolve_current_identity` call in a try/except and return `{}` (or just `{"author_email": ...}` without author_id) on any exception, so `attribution_fields` inherits the same NEVER-raise guarantee as `author_email()`.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `_git_config` NEVER raises (explicit list: OSError / non-zero exit / timeout), but provides no equivalent exception-handling rule for the `resolve_current_identity(repo_root=repo_root)` call inside `attribution_fields()`. The ddbe spec states the resolver never raises (all paths return None), but 4e9d never restates this contract or specifies a defensive guard inside `attribution_fields`. If ddbe's resolver raises unexpectedly (e.g., a future implementation change, a file-system permission error not covered by its current spec), `attribution_fields()` propagates the exception and breaks every mutating command.
findings.location: §1 Shared attribution helper — `attribution_fields` specification; AC4 (never-raise guarantee)
findings.suggested_fix: In §1, add: 'Wrap the resolve_current_identity call in try/except Exception: on any exception, treat it as None (omit author_id). attribution_fields() itself NEVER raises.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For the compact.py site, specify how `repo_root` reaches `attribution_fields` inside `_compact_locked` and `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` — either add a `repo_root` parameter to both functions (and update their callers: `compact_cli`, `compact_all_cli`, `fsck_recover` etc.) or document using `os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(tracker))` as the canonical derivation.
findings.finding: `_compact_locked` and `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` have no `repo_root` parameter today, yet the plan instructs calling `attribution_fields(repo_root)` inside both. The plan does not specify how `repo_root` arrives: neither adding it as a new parameter to these functions nor deriving it from the already-present `tracker` string (via `os.path.dirname(tracker)`) is mentioned. Without this, the implementer must silently invent the plumbing.
findings.location: Section 2 (4th site: compact.py), file_impact entry for compact.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add `repo_root: str | None = None` to both functions and derive it as `repo_root or os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(tracker))` inside each; update `compact_cli` to pass its existing `repo_root` param through to `_compact_locked`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that `_git_config` must ADD a `timeout=` to the subprocess call (not merely extract the existing inline code), and specify the timeout value (e.g. 2s matching the pattern used elsewhere in the codebase).
findings.finding: The existing `author()` function in `_seam.py` does NOT use a `timeout=` argument in its `subprocess.run` call (line 134: `subprocess.run(['git', 'config', 'user.name'], capture_output=True, text=True, check=False)` — no timeout). The plan specifies the new `_git_config` helper should 'return fallback on … timeout', implying a timeout parameter is needed, but this is new behavior not yet in the existing code. The plan's description of extracting `_git_config` 'from the existing `author()`' may mislead implementers into thinking timeout handling already exists.
findings.location: Section 1 (Shared attribution helper) — `_git_config` extraction spec
findings.suggested_fix: Add `timeout=2` to the subprocess.run call in `_git_config`, and catch `subprocess.TimeoutExpired` alongside `OSError` in the except clause.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `compact._git_author()` will be replaced by `_seam.author()` as part of this story (eliminating the duplication the `_git_config` extraction is meant to fix), or explicitly document why it must remain separate.
findings.finding: `compact._git_author()` is a near-duplicate of `_seam.author()` (both run `subprocess.run(['git', 'config', 'user.name'])`), yet the plan introduces `_git_config` as a unifying helper in `_seam.py` while leaving `compact._git_author()` alive. At compaction time the plan calls both `_git_author()` (for the `author` envelope field) and `attribution_fields()` (for `author_email`/`author_id`), keeping two separate implementations of the same git-config lookup in the codebase.
findings.location: What §2 (compact.py site) / `src/rebar/_commands/compact.py:274`
findings.suggested_fix: In the What §2 compact.py description, state that `_git_author()` in compact.py is replaced by a call to `_seam.author()` for the `author` field, so `_git_config` truly becomes the single git-config subprocess abstraction. This makes the Rule-of-Three for `_git_config` unambiguous (3 callers: `author()`, `author_email()`, and — via `author()` — compact's SNAPSHOT path).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command or patch target for each AC — at minimum: the pytest module/test name pattern to run (e.g., `pytest tests/test_attribution.py -v`), the mock target for AC4 (e.g., `patch('rebar._commands._seam.subprocess.run')`), and a `jq -e` expression for AC1's raw-event check.
findings.finding: No AC provides a concrete proving command (e.g., a pytest invocation, a jq expression against the written event file, or a shell one-liner) — all five ACs describe what to assert in prose but leave the executor to invent the verification mechanism, creating a gap between the plan's claimed verifiability and its actual specification.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all five ACs

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out note (e.g., 'new-event fields are silently ignored by older reducers — safe to roll back at any point; no migration needed') so deployment operators and downstream story authors (bde5, 3183) have a documented expectation.
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges back-compat for old events read by new code (AC3) but never states what happens when NEW events (carrying `author_email`/`author_id`) are read by OLD code after a rollback, nor provides an explicit back-out plan. Because events are permanently stored, a deployment rollback leaves `author_email`-stamped events in the log that old reducers silently ignore — this is safe but the plan relies on the reader inferring it rather than asserting it as the rollback strategy.
findings.location: Section 3 (back-compat) / AC3 / Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Deployment & rollback' paragraph to the Scope or Context section: 'Events stamped after deploy carry `author_email`/`author_id`; pre-rollback events already written retain these fields permanently. Old reducers ignore unknown envelope keys, so rolling back the code is safe — reduced state simply omits attribution for those events, matching pre-feature behavior. No migration or compensating event is required.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a design-doc or CLAUDE.md update (or named ADR) capturing: (a) the repo-keyed cache strategy and why `lru_cache(maxsize=1)` was rejected, (b) the 'omit `author_id` when None' contract, and (c) the role of `attribution_fields()` as the substrate for bde5/3183.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — process-scoped, repo-keyed denormalized attribution caching as a substrate for authorship attestation — but does not call for updating any ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc. Future maintainers encountering `_ATTRIBUTION_CACHE`, the canonical-path keying strategy, and the 'omit author_id when None' contract will have no persistent documentation explaining the design choices or their downstream significance (bde5, 3183).
findings.location: Section 1 (attribution_fields + _ATTRIBUTION_CACHE) / file_impact: _seam.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the title or Context section that `author` (name) is PRE-EXISTING and unchanged — only `author_email` and optionally `author_id` are new fields being added — so implementers do not accidentally introduce a new `author_name` key.
findings.finding: The plan title 'event attribution (name+email + optional author_id)' implies that author NAME is a newly-added field alongside email, but the plan body clarifies that `author` (name) is already stamped at every composer site via the existing `author()` call — only `author_email` and `author_id` are genuinely new. An implementer reading the title/context section first could mistakenly introduce a new redundant `author_name` field or become confused about what 'name' refers to in the attribution context.
findings.location: Context section / title; Section 1 `attribution_fields` definition
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Context / title phrasing to: 'Stamp author_email (and optional author_id) onto every mutating event envelope alongside the existing denormalized author (name) field' — making explicit that name stamping is not new work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that ddbe has shipped (or will ship before 4e9d merges) `identity.resolve_current_identity(*, repo_root=None)` with the exact keyword-only signature the plan assumes; document the actual import path and add a fallback/guard for ImportError in `attribution_fields` so a missing ddbe degrades gracefully rather than raising.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `identity.resolve_current_identity(repo_root=repo_root)` is shipped by ddbe with a keyword-only `(*, repo_root=None)` signature, but this symbol does not exist anywhere in the repo and is UNRESOLVED in the installed environment. The plan states '4e9d depends on ddbe' and treats this as a settled contract, but if ddbe has not yet shipped this exact API the entire `attribution_fields()` / `author_id` path cannot be implemented as specified.
findings.location: Section 1 (Shared attribution helper) — `attribution_fields` definition; also AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the import in a try/except ImportError inside `attribution_fields` (consistent with the plan's 'Omit author_id when None' intent): `try: from identity import resolve_current_identity; _id = resolve_current_identity(repo_root=repo_root) except (ImportError, Exception): _id = None`. Also pin the expected ddbe ticket/PR so reviewers can verify the dependency is ready.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fully-qualified import path for `resolve_current_identity` (e.g. `from rebar.identity import resolve_current_identity` or `from rebar._commands.identity import resolve_current_identity`) so the call site in `attribution_fields` is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan references `identity.resolve_current_identity(repo_root=repo_root)` implying a module named `identity` is importable, but the ddbe dependency ticket specifies only that a `resolve_current_identity(*, repo_root=None)` function ships in a 'new identity module' without naming its import path. If ddbe places the function at, say, `rebar.identity.resolve_current_identity` or `rebar._commands.identity.resolve_current_identity`, the call site in `attribution_fields` must use the exact path — but the plan does not name it, leaving the implementer to guess.
findings.location: Section 1 (Shared attribution helper), attribution_fields description
findings.suggested_fix: Check the ddbe ticket's `file_impact` section for the exact module path of the new identity module and write `from <that path> import resolve_current_identity` in the plan's Section 1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the txn.py line-number references: `transition_core` is defined at ~155 (STATUS event composed ~276); `claim_core` is defined at ~388 (STATUS event ~445, EDIT event ~462).
findings.finding: The plan's line-number references for `transition_core` ('STATUS ~280') and `claim_core` ('STATUS ~449, EDIT ~468') are substantially off from the actual code. `transition_core` is defined at line 155 and the STATUS event composition is at ~264-284; `claim_core` is defined at line 388 and the STATUS/EDIT compositions are at ~445-474. This won't cause a functional failure but may confuse implementers navigating the file.
findings.location: Section 2 (txn.py description), lines '_commands/txn.py — transition_core (STATUS ~280) and claim_core (STATUS ~449, EDIT ~468)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the exact import path and `(*, repo_root=None)` keyword-only signature of `resolve_current_identity` delivered by ddbe, and add the full qualified import (e.g. `from rebar.identity import resolve_current_identity`) explicitly to the plan so the implementer has a verified contract rather than a cross-ticket assertion.
findings.finding: The plan makes a specific, unverified capability assertion: 'ddbe ships this keyword-only (*, repo_root=None) signature' for `identity.resolve_current_identity`. A codebase search confirms `resolve_current_identity` does not yet exist anywhere in the repo (ddbe is listed as 'closed' but the resolver is absent). If ddbe actually ships a different signature (positional `repo_root`, or the function lands in a different module path), `attribution_fields` will fail at call-sites or silently receive the wrong argument.
findings.location: What § 1 (Shared attribution helper) — 'ddbe ships this keyword-only (*, repo_root=None) signature; 4e9d depends on ddbe'
findings.suggested_fix: Check the ddbe implementation for the module path (e.g. `src/rebar/identity/__init__.py` or `src/rebar/identity/resolver.py`) and add `from rebar.<module> import resolve_current_identity` explicitly to the `attribution_fields` implementation block in the plan, pinning both the import path and the verified signature.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback-state assertion: specify that a pre-change reducer reading a post-change SNAPSHOT (with author_email/author_id in compiled_state) either (a) silently ignores the unknown keys (cite the exact code path that guarantees this), or (b) document a compensating-forward strategy if rollback after compaction is not safe — attach a DATA LOSS RISK note if the latter.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the schema STATE after rollback when new-code compaction has already written SNAPSHOTs that embed author_email/author_id inside compiled_state. Rolling back the code leaves those SNAPSHOTs on disk; the plan only argues 'older reducers ignore unknown keys' in the forward direction (new code, old events) but never asserts that a pre-change reducer silently tolerates author_email/author_id baked into the SNAPSHOT's compiled_state blob — a distinct and unverified path.
findings.location: Section 3 (Replay back-compat) / Scope / AC3 — 'SNAPSHOT restores compiled_state verbatim (additive; older reducers ignore unknown keys)'
findings.suggested_fix: Cite the exact line in process_snapshot (or the SNAPSHOT loader) that merges compiled_state verbatim into the state dict without key-whitelisting, confirming old reducers are tolerant. If process_snapshot does `state.update(compiled_state)` unconditionally, that is the guarantee — name it in the plan. Add an AC entry: 'A SNAPSHOT produced by new code reduces without error under the pre-change reducer, and compiled_state contains no corrupted fields (asserted by running the old reducer against a new-code SNAPSHOT in a back-compat test).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the concrete Python import statement for `resolve_current_identity` to §1 (e.g. `from rebar._commands.identity import resolve_current_identity`) so the implementer has a fully-specified access protocol without needing to read ddbe's implementation.
findings.finding: The plan references `identity.resolve_current_identity(repo_root=repo_root)` throughout §1 but never specifies the concrete Python import binding that `_seam.py` must use. The ddbe `file_impact` only states 'new identity module' without naming a module path (e.g. `from rebar._commands.identity import resolve_current_identity` or `from rebar.identity import resolve_current_identity`). A source-code search confirms `resolve_current_identity` does not yet exist in `src/`, making the access protocol a referenced-but-undefined symbol.
findings.location: §1 Shared attribution helper (`src/rebar/_commands/_seam.py`) — `attribution_fields` definition; ddbe file_impact
findings.suggested_fix: Add to §1: 'Import as `from rebar._commands.identity import resolve_current_identity` (the module path ddbe ships the resolver under — confirm against ddbe's actual file_impact once it lands).'

findings.finding: The plan claims its list of four envelope-composition sites is 'verified complete,' but a fifth independent site exists: `_write_event_file()` in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py` (line ~214). It composes full event envelopes (timestamp/uuid/event_type/env_id/author/data) through its own `_event_meta()` helper — entirely separate from `append_event`, `txn.py`, `delete.py`, and `compact.py`. Inbound-reconciler events (Jira-pull CREATEs, STATUS updates, COMMENTs, EDITs) written via this path will never carry `author_email` or `author_id`, violating the AC1 claim that EVERY mutating path stamps `author_email`.
findings.location: What / Section 2 ('Stamp at ALL FOUR envelope-composing sites (verified complete list)'); file_impact list
findings.suggested_fix: In `_write_event_file`, after composing `event`, call `attribution_fields(repo_root)` (where `repo_root` can be derived from `tracker_dir.parent`) and merge the result: `event.update(attribution_fields(str(tracker_dir.parent)))`. Add `inbound_translate.py` to the file_impact list.

findings.finding: The reconciler's `_write_event_file` in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py` is an unenumerated fifth event-envelope composer: it constructs the full `{timestamp, uuid, event_type, env_id, author, data}` envelope and is called by `apply_inbound.py`, `apply_inbound_records.py`, and `applier.py` outside the `_commands/` directory. The plan claims a 'verified complete list' of 4 composer sites but omits this one.
findings.location: Section 2 / 'Stamp at ALL FOUR envelope-composing sites (verified complete list)'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
